fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Basic properties of positivity
The positive elements of a pointed dcpo

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} then I is inhabited. We will write \mathsf { pos } \, {u} to mean that u is a positive element.
(This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The bottom element is not positive

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The bottom element \bot of a pointed dcpo A is not positive.

Suppose that \bot was positive. The empty set { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} \subseteq A is semidirected and \bot \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} = \bot, but nonetheless { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} is not inhabited.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity is upward-closed

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let i \sqsubseteq j in a pointed dcpo A such that i is positive; then j is positive.

Fixing semidirected J \subseteq A such that j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}, we must show that J is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that i is positive becuase i \sqsubseteq j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Closure of positivity under directed suprema

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Omega } is closed under directed suprema.

Let J \subseteq A be a directed subset such that every u \in J is positive; we must show that \bigsqcup {J} is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset I \subseteq A such that \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I, we must check that I is inhabited. The inequality \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I holds if and only if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} for all u \in u; as J is directed, we therefore have some positive u \in A such that u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} from which it follows that I is inhabited.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The subdcpo spanned by positive elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet A^+ \subseteq A spanned by positive is a dcpo.
This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The positive part of a semidirected subset is semidirected

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let I \subseteq A be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo A; then the intersection I \cap A^+ is semidirected, where A^+ is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements.

Let i,j be two positive elements of I; we must show that there exists a positive element k \in I such that i \sqsubseteq k and j \sqsubseteq k. This follows immediately from the directedness of I and the fact that positivity is monotone.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity = definedness in a lifted dcpo

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo \mathsf { L } {A} are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ \mathsf { L } {A} }.

Let a \in A be an element of the dcpo A. To show that \eta _A a is positive, we fix a semidirected subset U \subseteq \mathsf { L } {A} such that \eta _A a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U to show that U is inhabited. The inequality implies that \top \sqsubseteq \exists u \in U. u{ \downarrow }, so we know that U is inhabited.

Conversely, suppose that u \in \mathsf { L } {A} is a positive element. Let U be the semidirected family { \mathopen {} \left \{ v \mid v = u \land u \downarrow \right \} \mathclose {}}; we evidently have u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {U}, so U is inhabited. Thus it follows that u is defined.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element a:A is positive if and only if for any partial element u: LA such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], we have u{ \downarrow }= \top.

Therefore, we first suppose that any u: LA is defined supposing a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] to check that a is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset I \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I}, we must show that I is inhabited. We consider the partial element u= { \mathopen {} \left ( \exists i \in I. \top , \lambda \_ . \bigsqcup {I} \right ) \mathclose {}} :LA, and observe that \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{i \in I} \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], and so we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] and thus it follows that u{ \downarrow }= \top and so I is inhabited.

Conversely, if a is positive, it follows that any partial element u is defined when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], because the subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ y \mid y=a \land u{ \downarrow } \right \} \mathclose {}} is semidirected.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity and the strictly-below relation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element a: A of a pointed dcpo A is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. \bot \sqsubset a.
(See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A general characterization of positivity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Sierpiński interval of a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal {M} be a dominion on \mathcal {C}. A Sierpiński space for \mathcal {M} is defined to be a universal \mathcal {M}-partial map, i.e. a morphism { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \top }}{ \Sigma } \in \mathcal {M} such that for any { U } \xrightarrow {{ i }}{ A } \in \mathcal {M} there exists a unique map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \chi _i }}{ \Sigma } making the following square cartesian:

fr:embedded-tex.fr:embedded-tex-body: \DiagramSquare { ne = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , se = \Sigma , east = \top \in \mathcal {M}, sw = A, nw = U, west = \mathcal {M} \ni i, south = \chi _i, south/style = {->,exists}, west/style = >->, east/style = >->, nw/style = pullback, }

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: When \mathcal {C} has an initial object \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } and every { \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ }}{ A } lies in \mathcal {M}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} where { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \bot }}{ \Sigma } is given as follows:

fr:embedded-tex.fr:embedded-tex-body: \DiagramSquare { ne = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , se = \Sigma , east = \top , sw = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , nw = \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } , south = \bot , south/style = {->,exists}, west/style = >->, east/style = >->, nw/style = pullback, }

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Under these circumstances, we refer to \mathcal {M} as a dominion that has a Sierpiński interval.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Paths in a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}. A path from { I } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ C } to { I } \xrightarrow {{ g }}{ C } in \mathcal {C} is defined to be a morphism { \Sigma \times I } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ C } such that the following diagram commutes:
We shall write p:f \leadsto g to denote a path from f to g.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positive elements in a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}, such that \mathcal {M}-partial map classifiers LA exist for all A \in \mathcal {C}. We will write \mathbb {L}= { \mathopen {} \left ( L, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on \mathcal {C}.

Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be an \mathbb {L}-algebra in \mathcal {C}, and let { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } be a generalized element of A. We say that { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is positive when for any { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } such that there exists a path from { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } to { C } \xrightarrow {{ u; \alpha }}{ A }, then { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } factors through some (necessarily unique) { C } \xrightarrow {{ y }}{ A } as below:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element x:A is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if { \mathbf {1} _{ } } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is a positive element in the dominion \mathcal {O} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos.
We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following:
fr:mainmatter.fr:blockquote: For any u \in LA, if a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: u{ \downarrow }}u[p] then u{ \downarrow }= \top.
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos. Therefore, our result follows from our earlier characterization of positive elements of a pointed dcpo in terms of partial elements.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity in constructive domain theory

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: These notes were prepared by Jon Sterling, based in part on discussions with Martín Hötzel Escardó and Tom de Jong. Any errors belong to Jon Sterling.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Basic properties of positivity
The positive elements of a pointed dcpo

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} then I is inhabited. We will write \mathsf { pos } \, {u} to mean that u is a positive element.
(This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The bottom element is not positive

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The bottom element \bot of a pointed dcpo A is not positive.

Suppose that \bot was positive. The empty set { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} \subseteq A is semidirected and \bot \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} = \bot, but nonetheless { \mathopen {} \left \{ \right \} \mathclose {}} is not inhabited.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity is upward-closed

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let i \sqsubseteq j in a pointed dcpo A such that i is positive; then j is positive.

Fixing semidirected J \subseteq A such that j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}, we must show that J is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that i is positive becuase i \sqsubseteq j \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Closure of positivity under directed suprema

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Omega } is closed under directed suprema.

Let J \subseteq A be a directed subset such that every u \in J is positive; we must show that \bigsqcup {J} is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset I \subseteq A such that \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I, we must check that I is inhabited. The inequality \bigsqcup {J} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I holds if and only if u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} for all u \in u; as J is directed, we therefore have some positive u \in A such that u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I} from which it follows that I is inhabited.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The subdcpo spanned by positive elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet A^+ \subseteq A spanned by positive is a dcpo.
This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The positive part of a semidirected subset is semidirected

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let I \subseteq A be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo A; then the intersection I \cap A^+ is semidirected, where A^+ is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements.

Let i,j be two positive elements of I; we must show that there exists a positive element k \in I such that i \sqsubseteq k and j \sqsubseteq k. This follows immediately from the directedness of I and the fact that positivity is monotone.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity = definedness in a lifted dcpo

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo \mathsf { L } {A} are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of { A } \xrightarrow {{ \eta }}{ \mathsf { L } {A} }.

Let a \in A be an element of the dcpo A. To show that \eta _A a is positive, we fix a semidirected subset U \subseteq \mathsf { L } {A} such that \eta _A a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U to show that U is inhabited. The inequality implies that \top \sqsubseteq \exists u \in U. u{ \downarrow }, so we know that U is inhabited.

Conversely, suppose that u \in \mathsf { L } {A} is a positive element. Let U be the semidirected family { \mathopen {} \left \{ v \mid v = u \land u \downarrow \right \} \mathclose {}}; we evidently have u \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {U}, so U is inhabited. Thus it follows that u is defined.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element a:A is positive if and only if for any partial element u: LA such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], we have u{ \downarrow }= \top.

Therefore, we first suppose that any u: LA is defined supposing a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] to check that a is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset I \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {I}, we must show that I is inhabited. We consider the partial element u= { \mathopen {} \left ( \exists i \in I. \top , \lambda \_ . \bigsqcup {I} \right ) \mathclose {}} :LA, and observe that \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{i \in I} \bigsqcup {I}= \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], and so we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p] and thus it follows that u{ \downarrow }= \top and so I is inhabited.

Conversely, if a is positive, it follows that any partial element u is defined when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p:u{ \downarrow }}u[p], because the subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ y \mid y=a \land u{ \downarrow } \right \} \mathclose {}} is semidirected.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positivity and the strictly-below relation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element a: A of a pointed dcpo A is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. \bot \sqsubset a.
(See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A general characterization of positivity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Sierpiński interval of a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal {M} be a dominion on \mathcal {C}. A Sierpiński space for \mathcal {M} is defined to be a universal \mathcal {M}-partial map, i.e. a morphism { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \top }}{ \Sigma } \in \mathcal {M} such that for any { U } \xrightarrow {{ i }}{ A } \in \mathcal {M} there exists a unique map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \chi _i }}{ \Sigma } making the following square cartesian:

fr:embedded-tex.fr:embedded-tex-body: \DiagramSquare { ne = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , se = \Sigma , east = \top \in \mathcal {M}, sw = A, nw = U, west = \mathcal {M} \ni i, south = \chi _i, south/style = {->,exists}, west/style = >->, east/style = >->, nw/style = pullback, }

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: When \mathcal {C} has an initial object \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } and every { \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ }}{ A } lies in \mathcal {M}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} where { \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } } \xrightarrow {{ \bot }}{ \Sigma } is given as follows:

fr:embedded-tex.fr:embedded-tex-body: \DiagramSquare { ne = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , se = \Sigma , east = \top , sw = \mathbf {1} _{ \mathcal {C} } , nw = \mathbf {0} _{ \mathcal {C} } , south = \bot , south/style = {->,exists}, west/style = >->, east/style = >->, nw/style = pullback, }

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Under these circumstances, we refer to \mathcal {M} as a dominion that has a Sierpiński interval.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Paths in a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}. A path from { I } \xrightarrow {{ f }}{ C } to { I } \xrightarrow {{ g }}{ C } in \mathcal {C} is defined to be a morphism { \Sigma \times I } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ C } such that the following diagram commutes:
We shall write p:f \leadsto g to denote a path from f to g.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positive elements in a dominion

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal {M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \mathopen {} \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}, such that \mathcal {M}-partial map classifiers LA exist for all A \in \mathcal {C}. We will write \mathbb {L}= { \mathopen {} \left ( L, \eta , \mu \right ) \mathclose {}} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on \mathcal {C}.

Let { \mathopen {} \left ( A, \alpha \right ) \mathclose {}} be an \mathbb {L}-algebra in \mathcal {C}, and let { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } be a generalized element of A. We say that { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is positive when for any { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } such that there exists a path from { C } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } to { C } \xrightarrow {{ u; \alpha }}{ A }, then { C } \xrightarrow {{ u }}{ LA } factors through some (necessarily unique) { C } \xrightarrow {{ y }}{ A } as below:

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element x:A is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if { \mathbf {1} _{ } } \xrightarrow {{ x }}{ A } is a positive element in the dominion \mathcal {O} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos.
We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following:
fr:mainmatter.fr:blockquote: For any u \in LA, if a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: u{ \downarrow }}u[p] then u{ \downarrow }= \top.
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos. Therefore, our result follows from our earlier characterization of positive elements of a pointed dcpo in terms of partial elements.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positive generation and open positivity predicates

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In a lifted dcpo \mathsf { L } {A}, the positive elements form an open subdcpo { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } {A} \right ) \mathclose {}} ^+ \cong A: thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of A^+ and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of A is the supremum of its “positive part”.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Open positivity predicate

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We say that a pointed dcpo A has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold:

fr:ol.fr:li: The positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Omega } tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { pos } }}{ \Sigma }.
The embedding { A^+ } \xrightarrow {{ \iota ^+ }}{ A } is a Scott-open immersion.
For a directed subset I \subseteq A, if \bigsqcup {A} is positive then there exists an element a \in I that is positive in A.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a dcpo; then an element a:A is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ b:A \mid b=a \land \mathsf { pos } \, {a} \right \} \mathclose {}}, i.e. we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Non-negatively generated elements

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: An element a of a pointed dcpo A is called non-negatively generated when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{ \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a holds.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated elements are non-negatively generated

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let a be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo A. Then a is also non-negatively generated.

We must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a = \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a, which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Bottom and non-bottom elements are non-negatively generated

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated.

fr:ol.fr:li: Any compact element a:A for which \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} holds.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Hence, any element a for which it is decidable whether a= \bot is non-negatively generated.

The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix a:A and assume \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}; we must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( a= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }a but the latter is equal to a.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated dcpos

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: When a pointed dcpo A satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated.

fr:ol.fr:li: Every element a: A is positively generated, i.e. we have a = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.
For any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, we have \bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: (2) implies (1) by setting I = { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \right \} \mathclose {}}. Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset I \subseteq A. We compute:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{a \in { \mathopen {} \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf { pos } \, a \right \} \mathclose {}} }a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} \bigsqcup _{p \in \mathsf { pos } \, a} a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} a

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated dcpos have open positivity predicates

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then A has an open positivity predicate.

Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset such that \bigsqcup {I} is positive; we must find a positive element of I. Because A is positively generated we know that \bigsqcup {I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( I \cap A^+ \right ) \mathclose {}}. Because \bigsqcup {I} is positive and I \cap A^+ is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Lifted dcpos are positively generated

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lift \mathsf { L } {A} is positively generated.

Let u= { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a \right ) \mathclose {}} be an element of \mathsf { L } {A}. Note that u is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \phi }u; as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have \phi = \mathsf { pos } \, u, and so we are done.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Lifted dcpos have open positivity predicates

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lifting \mathsf { L } {A} has an open positivity predicate.
It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: When is a continuous pointed dcpo positively generated?

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A such that each element of B is non-negatively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.

We must show that for any a \in A, we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a. Let B_a be the subset { \mathopen {} \left \{ b \in B \mid b \ll a \right \} \mathclose {}}. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a. Fixing b \in B_a, we must check that b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a.

As b is assumed to be non-negatively generated, we have b= \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} }b so to check b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a it suffices to check that \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}} implies that a is positive. We fix semidirected J \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup {J} = \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup J \right ) \mathclose {}} to check that J is inhabited. We recall that b \ll a and a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \mathopen {} \left ( { \mathopen {} \left \{ \bot \right \} \mathclose {}} \cup J \right ) \mathclose {}}, so it follows by the definition of the way-below relation that either b \sqsubseteq \bot or there exists j \in J such that b \sqsubseteq j. The first case is contradictory by our assumption that \lnot { \mathopen {} \left ( b= \bot \right ) \mathclose {}}, and the second case verifies that J is inhabited, so a is positive.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Basis with decidable bottomhood yields positive generation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A for each element b \in B it is decidable whether b= \bot. Then A is positively generated.
We observe that every element of B is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that A is positively generated.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated basis yields positive generation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A whose elements are all positively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.
As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that A is positively generated.
I am not sure how useful the following construction is. On the other hand, maybe it has a universal property at the level of posets.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The positively generated core of a dcpo

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the subposet A ^{ \mathit {pg} } \subseteq {A} spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of A.

Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum \bigsqcup {I} is positively generated, i.e. \bigsqcup {I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, \bigsqcup {I}} \bigsqcup {I}. Fixing i \in I, we must check that i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, { \bigsqcup {I}}} \bigsqcup {I}. As i is positively generated, we have i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, {i}}i. As positivity is upward-closed, we have \mathsf { pos } \, {i} \sqsubseteq \mathsf { pos } \, \bigsqcup {I}. Therefore, we deduce i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, i}i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, { \bigsqcup {I}}} \bigsqcup {I}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Characterizing freeness in terms of positivity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: In classical mathematics, every \mathbb {L}-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo A is positively generated if and only the evident map { \mathsf { L } {A^+} } \xrightarrow {{ }}{ A } is an isomorphism.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The “purification” map

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send a \in A to the partial element { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { pos } \, {a}, \lambda p. a \right ) \mathclose {}}; we note:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathsf { purify } _Aa = { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { pos } \, {a}, \lambda p. a \right ) \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a} \eta _Aa
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The mapping defined above is continuous because we have assumed that A has an open positivity predicate, so its domain of definition defines a Scott-open subdcpo of A.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Pointed dcpos with open positivity predicates are almost free

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A.

We fix { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right ) \mathclose {}} : \mathsf { L } {A^+} and compute explicitly using the fact that \mathsf { purify } _A preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathsf { purify } _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+} { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} & = \mathsf { purify } _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ & = \mathsf { purify } _A \bigsqcup _{p: \phi } a^+p \\ & = \bigsqcup _{p: \phi } \mathsf { purify } _A { \mathopen {} \left ( a^+p \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ & = \bigsqcup _{p: \phi } \bigsqcup _{q: \mathsf { pos } { \mathopen {} \left ( a^+p \right ) \mathclose {}} } a^+p \\ & = \bigsqcup _{p: \phi } a^+p \\ & = { \mathopen {} \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right ) \mathclose {}} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For the deflation, we fix a:A and observe that \alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { purify } _A a \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a} a which is evidently below a.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Positively generated pointed dcpos are free

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then A is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements A^+. More specifically, the embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv { A } \xrightarrow {{ \mathsf { purify } _A }}{ \mathsf { L } {A^+} } given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos.

First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A exists because A has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed.

Because { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right ) \mathclose {}} \dashv \mathsf { purify } _A is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that \mathsf { purify } _A; \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A is an inflation, i.e. for all a:A we must have a \sqsubseteq \alpha _A { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { L } { \iota ^+} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf { purify } _Aa \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}}. As the latter is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf { pos } \, a}a, the inflation condition follows from our assumption that A is positively generated.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Liftedness = positive generation

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A pointed dcpo A is lifted if and only if it is positively generated.
This follows from two existing lemmas:

fr:ol.fr:li: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated.
Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Domain theory in constructive and predicative univalent foundations

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We develop domain theory in constructive and predicative univalent foundations (also known as homotopy type theory). That we work predicatively means that we do not assume Voevodsky’s propositional resizing axioms. Our work is constructive in the sense that we do not rely on excluded middle or the axiom of (countable) choice. Domain theory studies so-called directed complete posets (dcpos) and Scott continuous maps between them and has applications in programming language semantics, higher-type computability and topology. A common approach to deal with size issues in a predicative foundation is to work with information systems, abstract bases or formal topologies rather than dcpos, and approximable relations rather than Scott continuous functions. In our type-theoretic approach, we instead accept that dcpos may be large and work with type universes to account for this. A priori one might expect that complex constructions of dcpos result in a need for ever-increasing universes and are predicatively impossible. We show that such constructions can be carried out in a predicative setting. We illustrate the development with applications in the semantics of programming languages: the soundness and computational adequacy of the Scott model of PCF and Scott’s D \infty model of the untyped \lambda-calculus. We also give a predicative account of continuous and algebraic dcpos, and of the related notions of a small basis and its rounded ideal completion. The fact that nontrivial dcpos have large carriers is in fact unavoidable and characteristic of our predicative setting, as we explain in a complementary chapter on the constructive and predicative limitations of univalent foundations. Our account of domain theory in univalent foundations is fully formalised with only a few minor exceptions. The ability of the proof assistant Agda to infer universe levels has been invaluable for our purposes.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Open locales and exponentiation
